title: University Life
description: Practice conversations about studying, campus life, and academic challenges

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: Come in! I'm Professor Hammond, your academic advisor. You must be Minh? Please have a seat.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Yes, thank you. I'm Minh. I appreciate you making time for me today.

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: Of course. So, what's on your mind? You mentioned in your email that you're reconsidering your course selection.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: That's right. I started my second year as a Computer Science major but honestly I've been struggling and I'm questioning whether it's the right fit for me.

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: That's a really important thing to reflect on. Can you tell me more about what's been challenging? Is it the content itself, the workload, or something else?

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: A bit of everything. I find the theoretical mathematics quite difficult — things like discrete math and algorithms. But I actually enjoy the practical parts, like building applications and working on user interfaces.

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: That's actually very useful self-awareness. Have you considered that you might be more aligned with a Software Engineering or Information Systems degree rather than pure Computer Science?

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: I didn't know those were different. What's the distinction?

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: Computer Science is more theoretical — algorithms, mathematics, AI fundamentals. Software Engineering focuses on the full software development lifecycle with less theoretical math. Information Systems combines computing with business, which is very employable.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Software Engineering sounds much more like what I enjoy. What would the process be to change majors?

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: You'd need to submit a change-of-major request form by the end of this semester. The good news is that most of your first-year courses count toward all three programs, so you wouldn't lose credit.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: That's reassuring. Would I still graduate on time?

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: If you switch now, likely yes — you're early enough in your degree. I'd map out your remaining requirements to confirm. Can you share your transcript so I can review what you've taken?

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Sure, here's my student ID so you can pull it up.

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: Let me see... You've completed Introduction to Programming, Calculus I, and Academic Writing. With a switch to Software Engineering, these all transfer. You'd need to add Software Requirements, System Design, and two practical project courses.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: That sounds very manageable. Can I also maintain my current GPA if I switch? I'm at 3.2 right now but I'm worried about dropping.

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: Many students actually find their GPA improves after switching to a program better aligned with their strengths. A 3.2 is solid — don't be too hard on yourself.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Thank you. That's encouraging. What should my next steps be?

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: First, attend an open class in Software Engineering next week — I'll arrange a visitor pass. Second, talk to two or three SE students about their experience. Then if you're sure, I'll help you complete the transfer form before the deadline.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: That plan makes a lot of sense. I really appreciate your guidance, Professor Hammond.

lines.speaker: Advisor
lines.text: It's what I'm here for. Choosing the right path is one of the most important decisions you'll make. Come back after the open class and we'll take the next step together.

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: Hey! You were in the Advanced Economics lecture, right? I'm Priya.

lines.speaker: Student B
lines.text: Yes! I'm Tom. I've seen you in there. That was a pretty intense lecture today, wasn't it?

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: Seriously. The section on monetary policy and inflation targeting went way over my head. Are you struggling with it too?

lines.speaker: Student B
lines.text: Absolutely. I was lost from about the third slide. I'm going to need to review it multiple times before the midterm.

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: I was actually thinking about starting a study group. A few of us have been talking about it. Would you be interested?

lines.speaker: Student B
lines.text: Definitely. I study much better with other people — explaining things out loud helps me understand them.

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: Exactly! I was thinking maybe three or four people maximum so it doesn't become chaotic.

lines.speaker: Student B
lines.text: I agree. A smaller group stays focused. I know Maya from our tutorial group who's quite strong on macroeconomics.

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: Oh, that would be great to have someone stronger in that area. My strength is more microeconomics and statistics.

lines.speaker: Student B
lines.text: And I'm decent at data interpretation and writing essays. Between us we'd cover most of the exam topics.

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: Perfect. When and where would work for you? I was thinking the library study rooms — they're quiet and have whiteboards.

lines.speaker: Student B
lines.text: The library works great. I'm available Tuesday and Thursday evenings after 6 PM, and Sunday afternoons. Are any of those good for you?

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: Tuesday evenings are perfect. I have a part-time job on Thursdays.

lines.speaker: Student B
lines.text: Tuesdays it is. Should we say 6:30 to 9 PM? That gives us 2.5 hours without it dragging on too long.

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: That's ideal. I'll book the study room for a recurring Tuesday slot. What should we prepare for the first session?

lines.speaker: Student B
lines.text: I think we should each review today's lecture notes and write down our specific questions. Then we can tackle them together.

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: Great idea. Should we also rotate who leads each topic? It forces everyone to actually understand it well enough to explain it.

lines.speaker: Student B
lines.text: Absolutely. That's a really effective technique. Okay — can we exchange contact details so I can add you to a group chat?

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: Sure. My number is 07882 344 110. I'll add Maya and let you know once she confirms.

lines.speaker: Student B
lines.text: Perfect. I'm looking forward to it. I feel better about the midterm already just knowing we have a plan.

lines.speaker: Student A
lines.text: Same here. See you Tuesday, Tom!

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Excuse me, I need to find academic articles for my dissertation but I'm not sure where to start.

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: Of course! Have you used our online database system before? It gives you access to millions of peer-reviewed journals.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: I've used Google Scholar, but I heard the university library has more resources.

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: We do. Log in to the library portal with your student credentials and you'll have access to JSTOR, Scopus, and Web of Science, among others. What's your dissertation topic?

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: It's on the impact of social media on mental health in teenagers.

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: For that topic, I'd suggest starting with PsycINFO — it's the best database for psychology research. Try searching for 'social media AND adolescent mental health' to get relevant results.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Should I limit the search to recent years?

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: For a topic this fast-moving, yes — filter by the last five years to get the most current evidence. You can also filter by peer-reviewed articles to ensure academic quality.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: How many sources do I need for a 10,000-word dissertation?

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: Typically 30 to 50 academic sources would be appropriate. Your supervisor will guide you, but quality matters more than quantity.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Can I come back if I need more help?

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: Absolutely. We also offer one-to-one research skills appointments. I'll give you the booking link — these are very popular so book early!

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Professor Chen, do you have a moment? I wanted to ask about the best way to prepare for the final exam.

lines.speaker: Professor
lines.text: Of course, come in. What specifically are you worried about?

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: I'm not sure whether to memorise the textbook or focus on the lecture slides. There's so much material.

lines.speaker: Professor
lines.text: Great question. The exam will test conceptual understanding more than rote memorisation. Focus on the big ideas and be able to apply them to new scenarios.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Should I work through past exam papers?

lines.speaker: Professor
lines.text: That's one of the most effective strategies. Past papers show you the format and the level of analysis expected. The library has copies going back five years.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Is it better to study alone or in groups?

lines.speaker: Professor
lines.text: Both have value. Study groups work well for discussing complex concepts, but make sure you also spend time on individual revision so you can identify gaps in your own understanding.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: I tend to panic in exams. Do you have any advice for that?

lines.speaker: Professor
lines.text: Before the exam starts, read all the questions carefully. Start with the one you feel most confident about to build momentum. And remember — you've prepared for this.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Thank you so much, Professor. That's really helpful.

lines.speaker: Professor
lines.text: Good luck. My door is always open if you need further guidance before the exam.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Hi, I'm having a problem with my student accommodation. My room has a broken radiator and it's been cold for a week.

lines.speaker: Accommodation Office
lines.text: I'm sorry to hear that. Can I take your student ID and room number?

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: It's Student ID 20234891, Room 14B in Langley Hall.

lines.speaker: Accommodation Office
lines.text: Thank you. I can see there's already a maintenance request logged. It looks like the repair team visited but the part they needed was out of stock.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Yes, they came but couldn't fix it. It's been really uncomfortable.

lines.speaker: Accommodation Office
lines.text: I completely understand. The replacement part has arrived and the team can come tomorrow morning between 9 and 11 AM. Will you be in your room then?

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: I have a lecture at 10. Could they come at 9 AM specifically?

lines.speaker: Accommodation Office
lines.text: I'll note that request. If you leave a key with the duty manager, the team can enter with permission even if you're not there.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: That works for me. Can I also request a temporary electric heater in the meantime?

lines.speaker: Accommodation Office
lines.text: Absolutely. I'll arrange for one to be delivered to your room this afternoon. Is there anything else I can help with?

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Will I receive any compensation for the inconvenience?

lines.speaker: Accommodation Office
lines.text: I'll flag your case for a review. Students affected by essential service outages lasting more than five days may be eligible for a partial rent credit. We'll contact you within 48 hours.
